data.title: Analysts Saw Metro 1Q Sales at EUR8.44B
A Quick Salmon Supper That Feels Like Spring — WSJ
data.description:
By Kitty Greenwald / Photographs by Elizabeth Coetzee/WSJ

The chef: Charlie Mitchell

His restaurant: Saga in New York City

What he's known for: Working his way from Detroit to New York City and climbing the fine-dining ladder; earning a Michelin star early in his career; taking the helm at Saga after his mentor, James Kent, passed away.

Charlie Mitchell had already stacked his résumé with fine-dining bona fides when his path took an emotional detour last summer. His mentor and friend, James Kent, unexpectedly died and Mitchell was asked to step in for him at the award-winning Manhattan restaurant Saga. Recalling one of their conversations, the chef said, "[Kent] told me to put my head down and do the work." Mitchell's recipe offers a glimpse of what that looks like.

Slow-roasted salmon, spicy cucumbers and a pea purée perfumed with herbs create a delightful contrast of textures. For perfectly cooked fish, Mitchell says, the devil's in the details. "You want the center just warm," he explained, warning against over-roasting. Rounded out by crunchy cukes and velvety peas, the dish brings brightness to the depths of winter.

  • Food styling by Dana Bonagura; Prop styling by Carla Gonzalez-Hart

data.title: Canada's Trade Surplus in December Provides Some Calm Before The Storm, Says Desjardins Economic Studies
data.description:
Canada's international merchandise trade balance went from a deficit of C$986 million in November to a surplus of C$708 million in December — the first surplus since February 2024, noted Desjardins Economic Studies.

Goods exports were up for a third consecutive month, increasing by 4.9% month over month, while imports rose by 2.3%. In real terms, exports were up by 2.4% month over month while imports grew by a more modest 0.6%.

The depreciation of the Canadian dollar (CAD or loonie) continued to boost nominal exports and imports for a third month in a row in December, wrote DES in a note.

The C$708 million trade surplus in December points to annualized real gross domestic product growth in Q4 2024 of 2.0%, slightly above the Bank of Canada's 1.8% from the January 2025 Monetary Policy Report.

However, Wednesday's trade release is the calm before the gathering storm of potential United States import tariffs in 2025, stated DES.

Looking ahead, DES expects the lingering threat of U.S. tariffs to increase volatility in both exports and imports, likely weighing heavily on growth and the minds of Canadian central bankers.

data.title: Danish Shipping Giant Mærsk Unveils DKK14.4 Billion Share Buyback Program
data.description:
A.P. Møller - Mærsk (MAERSK-A.CO) said postmarket Wednesday it will launch a 12-month share buyback program worth up to 14.4 billion Danish kroner.

The shipping giant earmarked 7.2 billion kroner in the program's first tranche, which will run from Feb. 7 to Aug. 6.

data.title: Mexican Peso Loses Ground Ahead of Banxico Decision
data.description:
The Mexican peso weakened to around 20.58 per USD, due to global trade tensions, mixed domestic economic indicators, and uncertainties surrounding U.S. monetary policy.

The persistent threat of U.S. tariffs, particularly US-China tensions, has heightened market volatility and sparked concerns over the stability of Mexico's export-dependent economy.

Meanwhile, risk aversion was compounded by the contraction of Mexico’s manufacturing sector, reflected in the manufacturing PMI of 49.1 in January, marking the sharpest decline in factory activity in three months.

Additionally, business confidence in Mexico remains subdued, suggesting a slowdown in economic momentum and contributing to downward revisions in growth forecasts and inflation expectations, further pressuring the peso.

On the U.S. side, robust employment data bolsters expectations of continued Fed tightening, diminishing the peso’s appeal in carry trades ahead of the anticipated rate cut by Banxico this Thursday.

data.title: US Equity Indexes Rise in Choppy Midday Trading
data.description:
US equity indexes rose in choppy midday trading Wednesday as investors weighed a deceleration in services with disappointing big-tech results overnight.

The Nasdaq Composite rose less than 0.1% to 19,676.6. The S&P 500 added 0.2% to 6,049.2, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 0.3% to 44,667.5 after both mainstream indexes declined earlier in the session. Consumer discretionary and communication services led the laggards, while real estate and technology were among the top gainers intraday.

Alphabet shares slumped 7% intraday after the company reported late Tuesday Q4 revenue that trailed analysts' estimates. Meanwhile, Advanced Micro Devices stock was nearly 7% lower after the chipmaker overnight reported weaker-than-expected data center sales for Q4 and guided to a sequential decline in Q1 revenue. Shares of the duo were among the worst performers on the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq.

In economic news, the Institute for Supply Management's US services index fell to 52.8 in January from 54 in December, compared with expectations for no change in a survey compiled by Bloomberg. The index indicates slower expansion, which is in line with the Richmond Fed, Dallas Fed, and the S&P Global indexes but in contrast with the New York Fed, Kansas City, and Philadelphia Fed measures that signaled contraction.

US Treasury yields fell, with the 10-year down 9.5 basis points to 4.42% and the two-year 4.6 basis points lower at 4.17%.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures dropped 2.1% to $71.16 a barrel.

US commercial crude oil stocks, excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, rose by 8.7 million barrels in the week ended Jan. 31 after a 3.5-million-barrel gain in the previous week and a 1.9-million-barrel increase expected in a survey compiled by Bloomberg.

data.title: RBB Bancorp Is Maintained at Neutral by Piper Sandler
data.description: (17:23 GMT) RBB Bancorp Price Target Cut to $20.00/Share From $23.00 by Piper Sandler

data.title: Oil Falls Amid U.S. Crude Build, Trade War Fears — Market Talk
data.description: Oil prices slide 2% despite U.S. President Trump reinstating maximum pressure on Iran, as rising U.S. crude inventories and fears of a trade war raise demand concerns. Brent crude is down 1.9% to $74.75 a barrel, while WTI falls 2.1% to $71.21 a barrel. The latest data from the Energy Information Administration show crude stockpiles rose by 8.7 million barrels last week, against expectations of a 1.3-million-barrel rise. Prices are also pressured by concerns that a trade spat between the U.S. and China could hurt global growth and raise inflationary pressures. "The oil market is now caught between increasing fears that an escalating trade war will damage global oil demand growth...and possible sudden disruption of Iranian oil exports," says SEB's chief commodities analyst Bjarne Schieldrop in a note. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Kennametal Shares Hit 52-Week Low After Disappointing 2Q Earnings
data.description:
By Chris Wack

Kennametal shares dropped 5% to $22.30 after the company reported second-quarter earnings that missed expectations, and cut its fiscal 2025 guidance.

The stock hit its 52-week low of $20.50 earlier in the session, and is down 9.5% in the past 12 months.

The industrial technology company said it had quarterly earnings of $17.9 million, or 23 cents a share, compared with $23.1 million, or 29 cents a share, in the same quarter last year. Analysts polled by FactSet were looking for earnings of $20.2 million, or 26 cents a share.

Adjusted earnings were 25 cents a share, compared with the 26 cents a share analysts had forecast.

Sales for the quarter were $482.1 million, compared with last year's $495.3 million and the $487.6 million analysts had expected.

The Pittsburgh company said conditions in a number of its end markets continued to weaken, resulting in sales at the lower end of its expectations, leading it to lower its fiscal 2025 projections.

Kennametal is forecasting sales of $480 million to $500 million in the third quarter with adjusted earnings of 20 cents to 30 cents a share. Analysts are looking for quarterly sales of $518 million.

For fiscal 2025, the company is now expecting sales of $1.95 billion to $2 billion, compared with previous guidance of $2 billion to $2.1 billion. Analysts are forecasting 2025 sales of $2.03 billion.

The company is also expecting 2025 adjusted earnings of $1.05 to $1.30 a share, down from the $1.30 to $1.70 a share it had previously been looking for. Analysts are expecting 2025 adjusted earnings of $1.44 a share.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: Teledyne Price Target Maintained With a $585.00/Share by Needham
Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1213 ET - Apple shares are under modest pressure amid reports that Chinese regulators are considering opening a formal investigation into App Store's fees and policies, even if revenue from the App Store in China is a manageable $5 billion, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. "So its less about revenue exposure for investors and more about building US/China tensions with US Big Tech in line for retaliatory shots across the bow," the analysts say, noting that China recently opened an antitrust probe into Alphabet's Google. "This is all a game of high stakes poker between Trump and China and Apple is clearly a chip on the table," the analysts add. Apple shares fall 1% to $230.39. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

0933 ET - ATS thinks that its worldwide scale can help jittery customers navigate U.S. tariffs. On the earnings call, executives say they have been discussing tariffs with customers who are cautious about the situation, but the company feels secure about its ability to support client supply chain complexities. ATS points to the company's global footprint as a big factor for stability--with a substantial presence in the U.S., Germany as well as Canada. "Conversations are more on the positive side, but clients are waiting to see how things will shake out," executives say. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0857 ET - Goldman Sachs economists think the risks of tariff escalation remain very real. The bank's team now projects that the U.S. will enact a 10% global tariff on critical imports such as semiconductors and pharmaceuticals as its baseline forecast, as well as a tariff on cars from the EU. If its current expected scenario is implemented, the tariffs could erase most progress on inflation otherwise expected this year, leaving core PCE inflation at 2.6% over the 12 months through December, Goldman projects. If Canada and Mexico tariffs are implemented after all, inflation would instead increase, peaking just above 3%, Goldman writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

0755 ET - Uber says it expects gross bookings in the current quarter of $42 billion-$43.5 billion, with the top end of that range coming in line with Wall Street's consensus view, according to FactSet. The 1Q outlook assumes a currency headwind to total growth of about 5.5 percentage points, the ride-hailing company says. Shares fall 5.3% premarket, despite 4Q results otherwise coming in ahead of Street views. (mark.long@wsj.com)

0518 ET - Google owner Alphabet reported weaker-than-expected growth at its cloud-computing business, AJ Bell's Dan Coatsworth writes in a research note. Google Cloud revenue grew to nearly $11.96 billion in the fourth quarter from $9.19 billion a year earlier, below a FactSet forecast of $12.16 billion. Weaker-than-expected growth in cloud computing suggests the AI frenzy isn't automatically translating into big bucks for infrastructure providers, Coatsworth says. Alphabet shares are down 7.4% premarket at $191.12. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0452 ET - Alphabet's capital-expenditures target for the year raises fears among investors that the Google owner might be wasting money, AJ Bell's Dan Coatsworth writes in a note to clients. Alphabet Chief Executive Sundar Pichai said the group expects to invest roughly $75 billion in capex this year. Investors would have welcomed large capex in the past as a sign that Alphabet was doing everything in its power to cash in on the AI frenzy, Coatsworth says. "Now the reverse is true," he says. "There are fears it might be digging itself a big hole and potentially wasting money if rivals like DeepSeek have shown it is possible to do things a lot cheaper." Alphabet shares are down 7.5% premarket at $190.91. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0433 ET - Shares of Google owner Alphabet are down Wednesday premarket after the tech giant posted revenue below market expectations for the fourth quarter. Alphabet stock closed 2.6% higher at $206.38 ahead of results on Tuesday. Shares fell in after-hours trading and are down 7% Wednesday premarket, touching a low of $191.10. Revenue in the fourth quarter grew 12% to $96.47 billion, below a FactSet forecast of $96.72 billion. Alphabet hasn't reported a growth rate that low since 2023. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0155 ET - Chinese AI company DeepSeek's breakthrough is unlikely to hurt demand in the short-term, although the market will likely cut multiples to price on uncertainties in the longer term, according to Julius Baer analysts in an emailed commentary. The market is debating on the long-term impact, as optimists argue that a lower entry barrier would attract new players, which should boost semiconductor demand, they write. "We do not expect much weakness in the near-term demand," the analysts note. They add that long-term demand will be determined by changes in AI-driven computing needs, such as inference, noting that such demand could offset the possibly lower computing demand in training. DeepSeek's development marks a major evolution, though not entirely a revolution, in artificial intelligence, they say. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

2237 ET - Inari Amertron's FY 2025-FY 2026 earnings my be pressured by weaker radio frequency revenue as handset sales slow down, CGS International analysts Shafiq Kadir and Dharmini Thuraisingam say in a note. Margin dilution from new packaging ventures could add pressure on earnings, they write, as CGS cuts its forecast for Inari's FY 2025-FY 2026 EPS by 19%-20%. However, margins may recover by FY 2026-FY 2027 with new businesses scaling up production, they add. CGS International lowers Inari's target price to MYR2.60 from MYR2.90, while maintaining a hold rating, citing near-term risks from a subdued automotive market and policy uncertainties under the Trump administration. Shares are 0.4% lower at MYR2.44. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)
